text: Document-level sentiment classification is a task which remains very challenging. It is hard to represent all semantic and syntactic relationships between sentences in a document, which affect the global sentiment of the document and indicates what the overall sentiment of the document is, whether it be positive or negative. Most of existing work makes use of sparse and discrete features, which only rely on local n-gram information, thus loses many global discourse relations, such as contrastive relation, causality, etc. In this paper, a unified, bottom-up neural network architecture, which composes the semantic representations of sentences to that of documents, is proposed. In this framework, a two-stage composition process is designed. First, features used to train a CNN or LSTM network in order to produce the semantic representation of each sentence. And second, these semantic representations are input to a GRNN which composes them into the document level representation based on a global view. In order to learn long-distance dependencies and to avoid the vanishing gradient problem, neural gates are adopted in the document composition layer. Experimental results on four large-scale review data from IMDB and the Yelp Dataset Challenge confirm that the proposed model outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines, including the feature-based SVM and Paragraph Vector. And the performance gain by applying gates to the document composition layer over using a normal recurrent layer.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
